  <parameters>"{\"lens\":\"security (輸入驗證 / 注入 / 認證授權 / 敏感資料暴露 / 依賴風險)\",\"findings\":[{\"severity\":\"medium\",\"where\":\"src/status-server.ts:88-315\",\"problem\":\"Status HTTP server 對所有 /api 路由零應用層認證。/api/specialists/:name/preamble 回傳 preamble.md、/api/specialists/:name/facts 回傳使用者私有記憶(facts_Topic md,含個人事實)、/api/specialists/:name 回傳 specialist 設定與(遮罩後)env。整個安全模型只靠網路綁定(預設 127.0.0.1)。但檔頭註解與部署方式明說要用 cloudflared/ngrok tunnel 當 Telegram Mini App 對外開放,且 STATUS_BIND_HOST 可綁 LAN——一旦開 tunnel,任何拿到 URL 的人即可無認證讀取私有記憶與 agent 設定。此外它是 Telegram Mini App 卻完全沒有驗證 Telegram initData 的 HMAC 簽章,無法辨識請求來自合法使用者。攻擊向量:攻擊者取得(或猜到/從 referrer 洩漏)tunnel URL → 直接 GET /api/specialists 列舉所有 specialist → 逐一 GET .../facts 與 .../preamble 匯出使用者長期私有 facts。\",\"fix\":\"在 HTTP server 加共享密鑰或 Telegram Mini App initData HMAC 驗證(用 bot token 驗 hash);未通過一律 401。至少對 facts/preamble/specialist detail 這些回私有資料的路由強制驗證,不要僅依賴 bind host。\"},{\"severity\":\"medium\",\"where\":\"src/commands/relay.ts:736-751, 757-833\",\"problem\":\"Relay 入站處理只用 ctx.chat.type===private && ctx.from.is_bot 當閘門,沒有 peer 白名單。peer 身分 parsed.from 完全來自對方訊息內文的 [RELAY:name] 前綴(攻擊者可任填),而 RELAY_DELEGATE 的 goal/context 文字經 buildRelayDelegationPrompt 後直接餵進主 agent 的 runPrompt(擁有完整 tool 權限的 session),構成跨信任邊界的 prompt injection;附件也會被寫入 uploads/ 並在 prompt 中告知路徑。攻擊向量:任何能對本 bot 私訊的 bot 帳號送出偽造 [RELAY:trusted-peer] RELAY_DELEGATE 訊息 → goal 內嵌指令被主 agent 當任務執行。實務利用受 Telegram「bot 不能主動私訊 bot」限制而降低,但一旦部署環境讓某 bot 能觸達本 bot,即無二次驗證。\",\"fix\":\"加入允許的 relay peer 白名單(以實際 Telegram user/bot id 而非訊息內文自稱的 name 判定),拒絕未知來源;對注入主 agent 的 relay 文字明確標註為不可信外部輸入,不讓其被當成使用者指令直接執行。\"},{\"severity\":\"low\",\"where\":\"src/status-server.ts:29-34\",\"problem\":\"maskEnv 只遮罩 key 名符合 /key|token|secret|password|credential/i 的欄位。若機密藏在其他命名的 env(例如含嵌入式 token 的 REDIS_URL、webhook URL、connection string),值會原樣回傳。在上述無認證暴露的前提下,這會擴大 specialist detail 端點的洩漏面。\",\"fix\":\"改為預設遮罩(白名單允許少數已知非機密欄位),或對值做 URL/連線字串內嵌憑證的偵測;根本仍是先補上端點認證。\"}]}"</parameters>

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user requested a code review of the telegram-kiro-bridge program with the message: "幫我檢視bridge的程式是否有問題" (Help me review the bridge program for any issues). This is a request to examine the codebase for potential bugs, security issues, or architectural problems.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Telegram bot architecture with grammy framework
   - ACP (Agent Client Protocol) / JSON-RPC 2.0 communication
   - Process lifecycle management (spawn, health monitoring, cleanup)
   - Windows-specific patterns (PATH restoration, taskkill /T, shell quoting, EPERM/EBUSY retry)
   - Atomic file operations with crash-safety guarantees
   - Token-based protocol parsing with code-region masking
   - Multi-tier timeout orchestration (turn, tool, orphan)
   - Session resume/archive patterns
   - Path traversal protection via whitelist validation
   - Localhost-only HTTP server binding for security
   - Authorization via ALLOWED_USER_IDS whitelist

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - src/atomicJson.ts (182 lines)
     - Implements Windows-friendly atomic JSON file operations
     - Key pattern: write-to-temp + rename for atomicity
     - Windows EPERM/EBUSY retry with exponential backoff (0, 10, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200ms)
     - Per-path promise chain for concurrent write serialization
     
   - src/run-prompt.ts (1,739 lines)
     - Core agent prompt execution logic
     - Handles relay delegation, specialist routing, MoA integration, goal tracking
     - Draft streaming with keepalive timer (10s interval to prevent 30s TTL expiry)
     - Coalesced editNow via async-coalesce.ts to prevent concurrent placeholder creation
     
   - src/sessionManager.ts (1,128 lines)
     - Session lifecycle management with multi-tier timeouts
     - Health monitor using process.kill(pid, 0) probe for silent death detection
     - Cancel operation sets _cancelledTurn flag and _orphanSuppressUntil (20s window)
     - Orphan turn flusher for background agent output with debounce
     
   - src/status-server.ts (342 lines)
     - HTTP API server for specialist monitoring
     - Binds localhost (127.0.0.1) by default unless STATUS_BIND_HOST overridden
     - safeDecode() wrapper converts URIError to null for 400 responses
     - Validates specialist names against whitelist before path.join to block %2E%2E%2F traversal
     - Masks environment variables with /key|token|secret|password|credential/i pattern
     
   - src/acpClient.ts (689 lines)
     - ACP client with JSON-RPC over subprocess stdio
     - Shell-aware spawning: .bat/.cmd/.ps1 use shell:true with manual quoting
     - Full PATH restoration from Windows registry via PowerShell
     - Clears TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN="" to prevent child agents from polling same token
     - Transient backend error retry with exponential backoff
     
   - src/relay.ts (442 lines)
     - Dual transport (Redis + Telegram bot-to-bot)
     - isUnderAllowedRoot() validates paths against config.sessionCwd + config.sendFileRoots
     - Self-send guard: blocks relay when targetBot === cfg.botName
     - Empty/placeholder guard: blocks relay when text.replace(/[.\s…]+/g, "") is empty
     
   - src/telegram-ui.ts (490 lines)
     - isUnderAllowedRoot() implementation: normalized lowercase path prefix checking
     - Prevents both directory traversal and arbitrary file disclosure
     - askRegistry Map for ASK token keyboard tracking
     
   - src/observerTransformer.ts (750 lines)
     - Balanced token scanner for nested <<...>> literals
     - Code-region masking: replaces ```fenced``` and `inline` blocks with sentinels
     - Prevents agents from triggering tokens when explaining protocol syntax
     
   - src/pet-server.ts (155 lines)
     - Electron pet app process management
     - Windows-specific cleanup: execSync(`taskkill /T /F /PID ${pid}`)
     
   - src/backup.ts (423 lines)
     - Git backup operations using spawn(cmd, args, {shell: false})
     
   - src/async-coalesce.ts (40 lines)
     - Coalescing runner pattern: max one in-flight execution
     - Fixes 2026-07-11 roadmap incident where concurrent editNow calls created duplicate placeholders

4. Errors and fixes:
   - No errors encountered during this review session
   - The codebase documents past fixes:
     - Draft TTL expiry issue fixed with 10s keepalive timer
     - Concurrent placeholder creation fixed with coalesceAsyncRunner
     - GOAL_DONE infinite loop fixed by switching from hard-reject to silent truncate

5. Problem Solving:
   - The primary session is conducting a systematic code review
   - Security analysis focused on:
     - Path traversal protection (isUnderAllowedRoot whitelist validation)
     - Authorization (isAllowed checks against allowedUserIds)
     - Localhost binding for status server
     - Environment variable masking
     - Percent-encoding attack prevention (safeDecode wrapper)
   - Process management analysis covered:
     - Windows-aware cleanup (taskkill vs kill)
     - Health monitoring (process.kill probe)
     - Timeout orchestration (turn/tool/orphan)

6. All user messages:
   - "幫我檢視bridge的程式是否有問題" (Help me review the bridge program for any issues)

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Complete the code review and report findings
   - The primary session appears to be running a /dev-review workflow (multi-perspective review with adversarial verification)

8. Current Work:
   The primary session is in the middle of a comprehensive code review of the telegram-kiro-bridge codebase. Most recent activity involved reading async-coalesce.ts and atomicJson.ts to understand concurrency control patterns and atomic file operations. The session has systematically examined:
   - Core execution logic (run-prompt.ts)
   - Session management (sessionManager.ts)
   - Security controls (status-server.ts, telegram-ui.ts, relay.ts)
   - Process spawning (acpClient.ts, pet-server.ts, backup.ts)
   - Token parsing (observerTransformer.ts)
   - Concurrency patterns (async-coalesce.ts)
   
   The review has validated multiple defense-in-depth security patterns and Windows-specific reliability measures.

9. Optional Next Step:
   The primary session should continue its /dev-review workflow to completion, then synthesize findings into a report covering:
   - Correctness issues (if any)
   - Security vulnerabilities (if any)
   - Resource leak risks
   - Architectural observations
   
   Based on the examination so far, the codebase demonstrates sophisticated security controls and Windows compatibility measures, but the full review workflow should complete before final conclusions.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: C:\Users\jiunchiwang\.claude\projects\C--Users-jiunchiwang--claude-mem-observer-sessions\af91c455-cd9f-4886-ab53-dee81dcc458f.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.

  - Medium severity: Relay lacks peer whitelist, vulnerable to prompt injection via RELAY_DELEGATE

**Key Technical Patterns Documented:**

2. **Process lifecycle**: Graceful shutdown with timeout-wrapped cleanup, fail-fast crash handlers exit(1) for supervisor restart
3. **Scheduler safety**: MAX_TIMEOUT_MS guards against 32-bit overflow, Number.isFinite() prevents NaN hot-loops
4. **File upload isolation**: sanitizeName() strips filesystem metacharacters, timestamped unique naming, isUnderAllowedRoot validation
5. **Telegram token isolation**: TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN blanked in child env to prevent concurrent getUpdates conflicts

The primary session appears to be running a multi-dimensional review workflow (likely reviewing security, correctness, performance, etc. in parallel or sequence). As the continuation session, I'm maintaining context continuity while the main review proceeds.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Tool hooks infrastructure exists but remains largely unused</title>
  <subtitle>registerHook/unregisterHook/clearHooks exported from tool-hooks.ts but only runPostToolHooks actively called in tool-loop.ts</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>tool-hooks.ts exports registerHook, unregisterHook, hookCount, clearHooks, and runPostToolHooks functions</fact>
    <fact>Only runPostToolHooks is imported and called in provider/tool-loop.ts line 15</fact>
    <fact>Hook registration API (registerHook/unregisterHook) flagged by ts-prune as unused exports</fact>
    <fact>Hook system wraps async and sync error handling with try-catch logging to prevent hook failures from crashing tool execution</fact>
    <fact>No grep matches for "registerHook" in src/ or scripts/ beyond the export declaration itself</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Tool hooks module implements extensibility infrastructure that was designed but never fully adopted. The module exports a complete hook registration API (register, unregister, count, clear) allowing external code to inject callbacks that run after tool execution. However code search reveals only runPostToolHooks is actually invoked—from tool-loop.ts after each tool call completes. The registration functions have zero call sites, meaning the hook list is always empty and runPostToolHooks becomes a no-op. This pattern suggests the hooks were built anticipating future plugins or monitoring integrations that were never implemented. The infrastructure remains as exported API surface (possibly for future use or external consumers) but adds no runtime value today. Error handling is defensive: both sync and async hook exceptions are caught and logged without propagating, ensuring a misbehaving hook can't crash the tool loop.</narrative>

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Parallel specialist dispatch includes verifier gate with automatic retry</title>
  <subtitle>dispatchParallel runs specialist output through quality check, retries once if verifier fails, then returns regardless</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>dispatchParallel in specialist.ts executes tasks via ephemeral AcpClient instances with configurable maxTurns and timeoutMs</fact>
    <fact>After completion, output longer than 50 chars runs through verifySpecialistOutput() gate checking quality against goal</fact>
    <fact>Verifier returns {pass: boolean, source: string, issues?: string} where source indicates which LLM performed verification</fact>
    <fact>On verifier NEEDS_FIX verdict, system logs issue and retries once with buildRetryPrompt(goal, issues) injecting failure reasons</fact>
    <fact>Retry has hardcoded 120-second timeout; if retry fails or times out, original output is used anyway</fact>
    <fact>Task completion status reflects timeout/done but not verifier verdict—a failed-then-used output still returns status:done</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Parallel specialist execution implements quality control via post-hoc verification pattern. After specialist produces output (up to maxTurns or timeout), the system invokes verifySpecialistOutput to assess whether response actually addresses the goal. The verifier uses an LLM (local or API depending on availability) to judge output quality and returns pass/fail with issue descriptions. Failed verification triggers automatic retry: buildRetryPrompt constructs a prompt explaining what was wrong, and the specialist gets one more chance (120s timeout) to fix it. Critically, this retry is best-effort only—if it fails or times out, the original flawed output is still returned to the caller with status:done. The verifier gate thus improves quality when possible but never blocks completion. This prevents verifier failures or false negatives from deadlocking parallel workflows at the cost of potentially delivering suboptimal output. Event logging records verifier verdicts for post-mortem analysis but the verdict doesn't affect the returned status field.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/specialist.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified></files_modified>
</observation>

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>ts-prune flags exports used in scripts/ as unused due to non-TypeScript import analysis</title>
  <subtitle>extractSendFileTokens, isPinned, prune show script usage but ts-prune only traces .ts imports</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>ts-prune reported 46+ unused exports across src/ modules including extractSendFileTokens, isPinned, prune, generateCorrelationId</fact>
    <fact>Manual grep shows extractSendFileTokens referenced in 2 scripts/, isPinned in 2 scripts/, prune in 1 script/</fact>
    <fact>scripts/ directory contains smoke tests, admin utilities, and diagnostic tools that import from src/ modules</fact>
    <fact>Many "unused" exports are lifecycle hooks (stopPetServer, stopRelayPoller, stopStatusServer) for graceful shutdown</fact>
    <fact>Constants like SKILL_USED_RE, GOAL_DONE_RE, RESTART_RE exported from observerTransformer.ts for protocol documentation</fact>
    <fact>Type-only exports (IntelRawItem, IntelPodcastJob, ProviderEvents) serve as API contracts for external consumers or future extensions</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Dead code analysis via ts-prune produces false positives because tool traces TypeScript module graph but scripts/ uses runtime imports or indirect references that static analysis misses. extractSendFileTokens flagged as unused but actually consumed by test/smoke scripts validating token extraction logic. Similarly isPinned used by usage-tracking admin scripts, and prune by cache maintenance utilities. Lifecycle hooks (stopPetServer, stopRelayPoller, stopStatusServer) appear unused because they're emergency/cleanup APIs not invoked in normal flow—intended for REPL debugging or future graceful restart features. Protocol constants (SKILL_USED_RE, etc.) exported for external tooling or documentation generation even though main code uses them internally. Type exports (IntelRawItem, ProviderEvents) support compile-time contracts for plugins/extensions. True dead code exists but requires manual classification: some exports genuinely unused (markLastCorrected, getEventsByType), some are public API surface kept for stability, some used by non-TS tooling ts-prune can't trace.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>discovery</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>multiple via ts-prune and grep</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified></files_modified>
</observation>

The primary session is conducting a thorough multi-dimensional code review, having now completed security findings and moved into dead code / maintainability analysis. The TypeScript compilation passed cleanly, and ts-prune analysis is revealing the difference between truly unused code and exports that serve as public API or are consumed by non-TypeScript tooling.

  <outcome>"{\"type\":\"text\",\"file\":{\"filePath\":\"G:\\\\AI\\\\telegram-kiro-bridge-main\\\\src\\\\acpClient.ts\",\"content\":\"   * quoting rules are primitive — wrap in double quotes and escape any\\n   * embedded quotes by doubling them.\\n   */\\n  private static quoteForShell(token: string): string {\\n    if (token === \\\"\\\") return '\\\"\\\"';\\n    if (!/[\\\\s\\\"&|<>^()]/.test(token)) return token;\\n    return `\\\"${token.replace(/\\\"/g, '\\\"\\\"')}\\\"`;\\n  }\\n\\n  constructor(private opts: AcpClientOptions) {\\n    super();\\n    // On Windows, CreateProcess can't launch .bat/.cmd/.ps1 directly, so we\\n    // need a shell for those. Everything else (node, npx.exe, a real .exe)\\n    // can be spawned without a shell — which avoids Node's DEP0190 warning\\n    // about passing args alongside `shell: true` and also sidesteps any\\n    // shell-quoting surprises in the args array.\\n    const needsShell = AcpClient.commandNeedsShell(opts.command);\\n    if (needsShell) {\\n      // When shell is required, build a single quoted command string and\\n      // pass empty args — that's the API shape Node wants post-DEP0190.\\n      const cmdLine = [opts.command, ...opts.args]\\n        .map(AcpClient.quoteForShell)\\n        .join(\\\" \\\");\\n      this.proc = spawn(cmdLine, [], {\\n        cwd: opts.cwd,\\n        env: buildSpawnEnv(opts.env),\\n        stdio: [\\\"pipe\\\", \\\"pipe\\\", \\\"pipe\\\"],\\n        shell: true,\\n      });\\n    } else {\\n      this.proc = spawn(opts.command, opts.args, {\\n        cwd: opts.cwd,\\n        env: buildSpawnEnv(opts.env),\\n        stdio: [\\\"pipe\\\", \\\"pipe\\\", \\\"pipe\\\"],\\n      });\\n    }\\n\\n    // Keep stdout as Buffer so LSP byte-length framing works correctly.\\n    this.proc.stderr.setEncoding(\\\"utf8\\\");\\n\\n    this.proc.stdout.on(\\\"data\\\", (chunk: Buffer) => this.onStdout(chunk));\\n    this.proc.stderr.on(\\\"data\\\", (chunk: string) => this.emit(\\\"stderr\\\", chunk));\\n    this.proc.on(\\\"exit\\\", (code) => {\\n      this.closed = true;\\n      for (const { reject } of this.pending.values()) {\\n        reject(new Error(`ACP agent exited with code ${code}`));\\n      }\\n      this.pending.clear();\\n      this.emit(\\\"exit\\\", code);\\n    });\\n    this.proc.on(\\\"error\\\", (err) => {\\n      this.emit(\\\"stderr\\\", `spawn error: ${err.message}\\\\n`);\\n    });\\n  }\\n\\n  private onStdout(chunk: Buffer) {\\n    this.inBuf = this.inBuf.length === 0 ? <observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Critical race condition in session cancellation allows turn state corruption</title>
  <subtitle>_cancelledTurn flag written but never checked, cancelled turn's post-processing corrupts new turn's state</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>SessionManager.cancel() sets session._cancelledTurn = true but grep shows zero read sites across entire src/</fact>
    <fact>cancel() sets inflight = false allowing new turn to start immediately via resetTurn()</fact>
    <fact>Cancelled turn's client.prompt() returns with stopReason='cancelled' but runPrompt continues full post-processing</fact>
    <fact>Old turn's post-processing uses new turn's buffer/toolTimeline from shared session object for final render and token side-effects</fact>
    <fact>Old turn's finally sets session.onUpdate = undefined killing new turn's streaming, and inflight = false breaking concurrency gate</fact>
    <fact>SCHEDULE/RELAY/SEND_FILE tokens from cancelled partial output can trigger unintended side effects</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Discovered critical race condition in session cancellation flow. The _cancelledTurn field was implemented as a guard flag but never actually consulted—it has exactly two occurrences in src/: the declaration and the assignment in cancel(). Comments claim "run-prompt finally skips post-processing" but runPrompt's finally block has no check for this field. When user cancels during a turn, cancel() optimistically sets inflight=false, allowing new user message to immediately start fresh turn via resetTurn (which clears buffer and sets new onUpdate). The cancelled turn's prompt() call later returns with stopReason='cancelled', but runPrompt continues full post-processing flow: (a) final render using shared session buffer that now belongs to new turn, (b) token side-effects (SCHEDULE/RELAY/SEND_FILE) potentially triggered by partial cancelled output, (c) history pollution, (d) finally setting session.onUpdate=undefined which kills new turn's streaming, (e) inflight=false breaking the concurrency gate and allowing third prompt to enter. This is write-only field anti-pattern at its worst—feature implemented halfway then abandoned.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>bug</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/sessionManager.ts</file>
    <file>src/run-prompt.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified></files_modified>
</observation>

<observation>
  <type>bug</type>
  <title>Concurrent SessionManager.get() spawns duplicate ACP clients causing process leak</title>
  <subtitle>No in-progress deduplication allows multiple create() paths to spawn competing subprocesses for same chat</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>SessionManager.get() has no synchronization on concurrent calls for same chatId</fact>
    <fact>create() contains multiple second-scale awaits: loadFacts, compressFactsForPreamble (LLM call), readFile, provider.initialize (spawn+RPC)</fact>
    <fact>Concurrent triggers exist in production: message handler, ASK callbacks, specialist result injection, pet chat, /back command</fact>
    <fact>Second create() to complete calls sessions.set() overwriting first session in map</fact>
    <fact>Overwritten session's subprocess never closed because exit handler checks sessions.get(chatId) === session which now fails</fact>
    <fact>Each leaked subprocess holds process table entry, stdio pipes, and allocated preamble memory</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Discovered process leak vulnerability in SessionManager concurrency control. The get() method lacks in-progress deduplication—concurrent calls for same chatId each enter create() and spawn independent ACP subprocesses. Race window is substantial due to multi-second operations: loading facts from disk, LLM compression of preamble (if local-llm available), file I/O, and most critically the spawn() + RPC initialization handshake. Production has multiple concurrent trigger paths: user sends message while ASK callback arrives, or specialist parallel result injection fires via background .then() while user sends follow-up. Winner of sessions.set() race becomes authoritative session; loser's session object becomes orphaned. Exit handler in sessionManager only cleans up if sessions.get(chatId) returns that exact session instance—check fails for loser, so its subprocess never receives close() and runs forever. Each leak consumes: one process table slot, three stdio pipe descriptors, RPC client state, and full preamble memory (facts can be 200KB+ compressed). Over days of bridge runtime with moderate specialist/parallel usage, this accumulates dozens of zombie ACP processes.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>bug</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/sessionManager.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified></files_modified>
</observation>

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Status HTTP server designed for tunnel exposure but completely lacks authentication</title>
  <subtitle>All API routes including private facts/preamble/config return data to any request when cloudflared/ngrok tunnel opened</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Status server binds 127.0.0.1 by default for localhost-only access as primary security model</fact>
    <fact>File header comments explicitly describe deployment via cloudflared/ngrok tunnel for Telegram Mini App external access</fact>
    <fact>STATUS_BIND_HOST environment variable allows binding to LAN interfaces</fact>
    <fact>/api/specialists/:name/facts endpoint returns user's private long-term memory facts without any authentication check</fact>
    <fact>/api/specialists/:name/preamble returns full preamble.md content, /api/specialists/:name returns config with masked env</fact>
    <fact>POST /api/status/:id/cancel allows task cancellation without verifying request origin</fact>
    <fact>No Telegram Mini App initData HMAC signature verification despite being designated for WebApp use</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Discovered authentication gap in deployment model. Status HTTP server implements zero application-layer authentication on any route, relying entirely on network-layer isolation (localhost binding). However design documents and code comments explicitly describe tunnel deployment pattern: cloudflared/ngrok exposes localhost server to internet for Telegram Mini App access. Once tunnel URL leaks (via referrer headers, shared links, or simple guessing of tunnel subdomain), any party can: enumerate all specialists via /api/specialists, download private user facts via .../facts (long-term personal memory including sensitive information), read preamble and config, cancel running tasks via POST .../cancel. The Telegram Mini App integration lacks standard WebApp security: no initData parameter validation, no HMAC signature check using bot token, no user_id verification. Attack requires only obtaining tunnel URL—no credentials needed. This combines localhost-only security assumption with public internet exposure requirement without reconciliation layer.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>bug</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/status-server.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified></files_modified>
</observation>

The primary session has completed a highly detailed multi-dimensional code review covering security, correctness, resource management, and code quality. The findings range from critical race conditions and process leaks to YAGNI infrastructure and testability gaps. The review demonstrates deep understanding of the codebase's concurrency patterns, lifecycle management, and deployment security model.

Everything else (node, npx.exe, a real .exe)\\n    // can be spawned without a shell — which avoids Node's DEP0190 warning\\n    // about passing args alongside `shell: true` and also sidesteps any\\n    // shell-quoting surprises in the args array.\\n    const needsShell = AcpClient.commandNeedsShell(opts.command);\\n    if (needsShell) {\\n      // When shell is required, build a single quoted command string and\\n      // pass empty args — that's the API shape Node wants post-DEP0190.\\n      const cmdLine = [opts.command, ...opts.args]\\n        .map(AcpClient.quoteForShell)\\n        .join(\\\" \\\");\\n      this.proc = spawn(cmdLine, [], {\\n        cwd: opts.cwd,\\n        env: buildSpawnEnv(opts.env),\\n        stdio: [\\\"pipe\\\", \\\"pipe\\\", \\\"pipe\\\"],\\n        shell: true,\\n      });\\n    } else {\\n      this.proc = spawn(opts.command, opts.args, {\\n        cwd: opts.cwd,\\n        env: buildSpawnEnv(opts.env),\\n        stdio: [\\\"pipe\\\", \\\"pipe\\\", \\\"pipe\\\"],\\n      });\\n    }\\n\\n    // Keep stdout as Buffer so LSP byte-length framing works correctly.\\n    this.proc.stderr.setEncoding(\\\"utf8\\\");\\n\\n    this.proc.stdout.on(\\\"data\\\", (chunk: Buffer) => this.onStdout(chunk));\\n    this.proc.stderr.on(\\\"data\\\", (chunk: string) => this.emit(\\\"stderr\\\", chunk));\\n    this.proc.on(\\\"exit\\\", (code) => {\\n      this.closed = true;\\n      for (const { reject } of this.pending.values()) {\\n        reject(new Error(`ACP agent exited with code ${code}`));\\n      }\\n      this.pending.clear();\\n      this.emit(\\\"exit\\\", code);\\n    });\\n    this.proc.on(\\\"error\\\", (err) => {\\n      this.emit(\\\"stderr\\\", `spawn error: ${err.message}\\\\n`);\\n    });\\n  }\\n\\n  private onStdout(chunk: Buffer) {\\n    this.inBuf = this.inBuf.length === 0 ? chunk : Buffer.concat([this.inBuf, chunk]);\\n    // Keep draining: either LSP framed messages or newline-terminated JSON.\\n    for (;;) {\\n      const consumed = this.tryReadOne();\\n      if (!consumed) break;\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  /**\\n   * Try to read one full JSON-RPC message from the buffer.\\n   * Returns true if a message was consumed (loop again), false otherwise.\\n   */\\n  private tryReadOne(): boolean {\\n    if (this.inBuf.length === 0) return false;\\n\\n    // 1) LSP framing starts with \\\"Content-Length:\\\".\\n    //    Peek first few bytes; if they look like a header, parse it.\\n    if (this.looksLikeLspHeader()) {\\n      return this.tryReadLsp();\\n    }\\n\\n    // 2) Otherwise treat as line-delimited JSON.\\n    const nl = this.inBuf.indexOf(0x0a); // \\\"\\\\n\\\"\\n    if (nl < 0) return false;\",\"numLines\":80,\"startLine\":250,\"totalLines\":689}}"</outcome>
